column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Rhenium Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/metal/non-ferrous/rhenium-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for Rhenium oxide removal without substrate damage
Near-IR wavelength for efficient Rhenium oxide absorption
Optimal spot size for precision Rhenium cleaning
High repetition rate for efficient Rhenium surface cleaning
Fluence threshold for Rhenium oxide removal
Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled Rhenium oxide ablation
Scan speed for efficient Rhenium surface coverage
Number of passes for complete Rhenium oxide removal
Optimal overlap for uniform Rhenium surface cleaning

Typical range for industrial cleaning of refractory metals like Rhenium to balance efficiency and avoid thermal damage; recommended starting at 150 W for 1064 nm Nd:YAG lasers. Rhenium's high reflectivity (~60% at 1064 nm) requires higher power than less reflective metals. Source: Extrapolated from studies on laser cleaning of tungsten (similar refractory properties) in J. Laser Appl. 32, 022064 (2020); manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics YLR series.

Range for effective contaminant removal without substrate ablation; slower for heavy contamination (e.g., oxides), faster for light surface prep. Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal balance on Rhenium to minimize heat accumulation given its high thermal conductivity (48 W/m·K). Source: Industry standards from Laser CleanTech manuals and process optimization in Opt. Lasers Eng. 128, 106010 (2020); conservative for Rhenium's properties.

Nanosecond regime typical for industrial cleaning to ablate contaminants while sparing Rhenium substrate; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-minimal HAZ but less common industrially due to cost. Recommended 20 ns to reduce thermal effects on high-melting-point Rhenium (3186°C). Source: Laser physics research in Appl. Surf. Sci. 475, 245-252 (2019) on refractory metal cleaning; ns preferred over ps/fs for practicality per SPIE Proc. Vol. 10911.

Typical for pulsed fiber lasers in cleaning; higher rates speed up process but increase heat buildup on reflective Rhenium, risking subsurface damage. Recommended 50 kHz balances speed and thermal management. Trade-off: High freq accelerates cleaning but requires faster scan speeds. Source: Academic literature in J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 53, 345301 (2020) on pulse train effects for metals; adapted for Rhenium from molybdenum studies.

Fluence range below Rhenium's ablation threshold (~4-5 J/cm² at 1064 nm) for safe contaminant removal; lower for light cleaning, higher for heavy. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² to avoid damage given Rhenium's high melting point and reflectivity. Damage threshold estimated >3 J/cm². Source: Ablation threshold studies in Appl. Phys. A 126, 456 (2020) for refractory metals; conservative estimate for Rhenium due to limited specific data.

Beam diameter range for precision vs. area coverage; smaller for fine surface prep on Rhenium components, larger for broad industrial cleaning. Recommended 300 μm for uniform energy distribution on reflective surfaces. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent Armonia series and Trumpf lasers; based on optimization in Surf. Coat. Technol. 384, 125347 (2020) for metal cleaning applications.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy or uneven layers; recommended 2 passes for complete Rhenium cleaning to ensure uniformity without excessive heat input. Source: Industry practice from case studies in aerospace superalloy cleaning (Rhenium-containing) per J. Manuf. Processes 52, 1-12 (2020); multi-pass reduces risk of incomplete removal.

Ensures uniform coverage without gaps or over-ablation; higher overlap for fine Rhenium surface prep to account for reflectivity variations. Recommended 70% for optimal uniformity in industrial settings. Source: Process optimization studies in Opt. Laser Technol. 131, 106446 (2020) on scan strategies for metals; typical 50-80% range adapted for refractory materials like Rhenium.
